subject: Chemistry
question_text: A piece of metal is 3 inch (represented by in) long. What is its length in cm?
question_html: A piece of metal is 3 inch (represented by in) long. What is its length in cm?
solution_text: We know that 1 in = 2.54 cm From this equivalence, we can write {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{in}}{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}=1=\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{in}}{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{/tex} equals 1 and {tex}\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex} = 1 Say, the 3 in given above is multiplied by the unit factor. So, 3 in = 3 in {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.54 cm = 7.62 cm
solution_html:
We know that 1 in = 2.54 cm
From this equivalence, we can write
{tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{in}}{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}=1=\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{in}}{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{/tex} equals 1 and {tex}\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex}= 1
Say, the 3 in given above is multiplied by the unit factor. So,
3 in = 3 in {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2.54 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{in}}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.54 cm = 7.62 cm

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A jug contains 2L of milk. Calculate the volume of the milk in m 3 .
question_html: A jug contains 2L of milk. Calculate the volume of the milk in m3.
solution_text: Since 1 L = 1000 cm 3 and 1m = 100 cm, which gives {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{m}}{100 \mathrm{cm}}=1=\frac{100 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{m}}{/tex} To get m 3 from the above unit factors, the first unit factor is taken and it is cubed. {tex}\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{m}}{100 \mathrm{cm}}\right)^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{1 \mathrm{m}^{3}}{10^{6} \mathrm{cm}^{3}}{/tex} = (1) 3 = 1 Now 2 L = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 cm 3 The above is multiplied by the unit factor 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000cm 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1 m^{3}}{10^{6} cm^{3}}=\frac{2 m^{2}}{10^{3}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m 3
solution_html:
Since 1 L = 1000 cm3
and 1m = 100 cm, which gives
{tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{m}}{100 \mathrm{cm}}=1=\frac{100 \mathrm{cm}}{1 \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
To get m3 from the above unit factors, the first unit factor is taken and it is cubed.
{tex}\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{m}}{100 \mathrm{cm}}\right)^{3} \Rightarrow \frac{1 \mathrm{m}^{3}}{10^{6} \mathrm{cm}^{3}}{/tex} = (1)3 = 1
Now 2 L = 2{tex}\times{/tex}1000 cm3
The above is multiplied by the unit factor
2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000cm3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1 m^{3}}{10^{6} cm^{3}}=\frac{2 m^{2}}{10^{3}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m3

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How many seconds are there in 2 days?
question_html: How many seconds are there in 2 days?
solution_text: Here, we know 1 day = 24 hours (h) or {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{day}}{24 \mathrm{h}}=1=\frac{24 \mathrm{h}}{1 \mathrm{day}}{/tex} then, 1h = 60 min or {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{h}}{60 \mathrm{min}}=1=\frac{60 \mathrm{min}}{1 \mathrm{h}}{/tex} so, for converting 2 days to seconds, i.e., 2 days ________ = ________ seconds The unit factors can be multiplied in series in one step only as follows: 2 day {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{24 \mathrm{h}}{1 \mathrm{day}} \times \frac{60 \mathrm{min}}{1 \mathrm{h}} \times \frac{60 \mathrm{s}}{1 \mathrm{min}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 s = 172800 s
solution_html:
Here, we know 1 day = 24 hours (h)
or {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{day}}{24 \mathrm{h}}=1=\frac{24 \mathrm{h}}{1 \mathrm{day}}{/tex}
then, 1h = 60 min
or {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{h}}{60 \mathrm{min}}=1=\frac{60 \mathrm{min}}{1 \mathrm{h}}{/tex}
so, for converting 2 days to seconds,
i.e., 2 days ________ = ________ seconds
The unit factors can be multiplied in series in one step only as follows:
2 day {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{24 \mathrm{h}}{1 \mathrm{day}} \times \frac{60 \mathrm{min}}{1 \mathrm{h}} \times \frac{60 \mathrm{s}}{1 \mathrm{min}}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 s
= 172800 s

question_text: Calculate molecular mass of glucose (C 6 H 12 O 6 ) molecule.
question_html: Calculate molecular mass of glucose (C6H12O6) molecule.
solution_text: The molecular mass of glucose (C 6 H 12 O 6 ) =6 {tex}\times{/tex} C + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} H + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} O = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} (12.011) + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} (1.008) + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} (16.00) =72.066 + 12.096 + 96.00 = 180.162 u
solution_html:

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A compound contains 4.07% hydrogen, 24.27% carbon and 71.65% chlorine. Its molar mass is 98.96 g. What are its empirical and molecular formulas?
question_html: A compound contains 4.07% hydrogen, 24.27% carbon and 71.65% chlorine. Its molar mass is 98.96 g. What are its empirical and molecular formulas?
solution_text: Step 1. Conversion of a mass percent to grams Since we are having a mass percent, it is convenient to use 100 g of the compound as the starting material. Thus, in the 100 g sample of the above compound, 4.07g hydrogen, 24.27g carbon and 71.65g chlorine are present. Step 2. Convert into number moles of each element Divide the masses obtained above by respective atomic masses of various elements. This gives the number of moles of constituent elements in the compound Moles of hydrogen = {tex}\frac{4.07 g}{1.008 g}{/tex} = 4.04 Moles of carbon = {tex}\frac{24.27}{12.01 g}{/tex} = 2.021 Moles of chlorine = {tex}\frac{71.65 g}{35.453 g}{/tex} = 2.021 Step 3. Divide each of the mole values obtained above by the smallest number amongst them Since 2.021 is the smallest value, division by it gives a ratio of 2:1:1 for H:C:Cl In case the ratios are not whole numbers, then they may be converted into the whole number by multiplying by the suitable coefficient. Step 4. Write down the empirical formula by mentioning the numbers after writing the symbols of respective elements CH 2 Cl is, thus, the empirical formula of the above compound. Step 5. Writing molecular formula (a) Determine empirical formula mass by adding the atomic masses of various atoms present in the empirical formula. For CH 2 Cl, empirical formula mass is 12.01 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008) + 35.453 = 49.48 g (b) Divide Molar mass by empirical formula mass {tex}\frac{\text { Molar mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}=\frac{98.96 \mathrm{g}}{49.48 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 2 = (n) (c) Multiply empirical formula by n obtained above to get the molecular formula Empirical formula = CH 2 Cl, n = 2 Hence molecular formula is C 2 H 4 Cl 2
solution_html:
Step 1. Conversion of a mass percent to grams
Since we are having a mass percent, it is convenient to use 100 g of the compound as the starting material. Thus, in the 100 g sample of the above compound, 4.07g hydrogen, 24.27g carbon and 71.65g chlorine are present.
Step 2. Convert into number moles of each element
Divide the masses obtained above by respective atomic masses of various elements. This gives the number of moles of constituent elements in the compound
Moles of hydrogen = {tex}\frac{4.07 g}{1.008 g}{/tex} = 4.04
Moles of carbon = {tex}\frac{24.27}{12.01 g}{/tex} = 2.021
Moles of chlorine = {tex}\frac{71.65 g}{35.453 g}{/tex} = 2.021
Step 3. Divide each of the mole values obtained above by the smallest number amongst them
Since 2.021 is the smallest value, division by it gives a ratio of 2:1:1 for H:C:Cl
In case the ratios are not whole numbers, then they may be converted into the whole number by multiplying by the suitable coefficient.
Step 4. Write down the empirical formula by mentioning the numbers after writing the symbols of respective elements
CH2Cl is, thus, the empirical formula of the above compound.
Step 5. Writing molecular formula
(a) Determine empirical formula mass by adding the atomic masses of various atoms present in the empirical formula.
For CH2Cl, empirical formula mass is
12.01 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.008) + 35.453
= 49.48 g
(b) Divide Molar mass by empirical formula mass
{tex}\frac{\text { Molar mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}=\frac{98.96 \mathrm{g}}{49.48 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 2 = (n)
(c) Multiply empirical formula by n obtained above to get the molecular formula
Empirical formula = CH2Cl, n = 2
Hence molecular formula is C2H4Cl2

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the amount of water (g) produced by the combustion of 16 g of methane.
question_html: Calculate the amount of water (g) produced by the combustion of 16 g of methane.
solution_text: The balanced equation for the combustion of methane is: CH 4 (g) + 2O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO 2 (g) + 2H 2 O (g) 16 g of CH 4 corresponds to one mole. From the above equation, 1 mol of CH 4 (g) gives 2 mol of H 2 O (g). 2 mol of water (H 2 O) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (2+16) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 = 36 g 1 mol H 2 O = 18 g H 2 O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{18 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 1 Hence, 2 mol H 2 O {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{18 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 g H 2 O = 36 g H 2 O
solution_html:
The balanced equation for the combustion of methane is:
CH4 (g) + 2O2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CO2 (g) + 2H2O (g)

  1. 16 g of CH4 corresponds to one mole.
  2. From the above equation, 1 mol of CH4 (g) gives 2 mol of H2O (g).
    2 mol of water (H2O) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (2+16)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 = 36 g
    1 mol H2O = 18 g H2O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{18 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 1
    Hence, 2 mol H2O {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{18 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 g H2O = 36 g H2O

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How many moles of methane are required to produce 22g CO 2 (g) after combustion?
question_html: How many moles of methane are required to produce 22g CO2 (g) after combustion?
solution_text: According to the chemical equation, CH 4 (g) + 2O 2 (g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO 2 (g) + 2H 2 O (g) 44g CO 2 (g) is obtained from 16 g CH 4 (g). [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol CO 2 (g) is obtained from 1 mol of CH 4 (g)] Number of moles of CO 2 (g) = 22 g CO 2 (g) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{44 \mathrm{gCO}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex} = 0.5 mol CO 2 (g) Hence, 0.5 mol CO 2 (g) would be obtained from 0.5 mol CH 4 (g) or 0.5 mol of CH 4 (g) would be required to produce 22 g CO 2 (g).
solution_html:
According to the chemical equation,
CH4 (g) + 2O2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} CO2(g) + 2H2O (g)
44g CO2 (g) is obtained from 16 g CH4 (g).
[{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 mol CO2(g) is obtained from 1 mol of CH4(g)]
Number of moles of CO2 (g)
= 22 g CO2 (g) {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{44 \mathrm{gCO}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex}
= 0.5 mol CO2(g)
Hence, 0.5 mol CO2 (g) would be obtained from 0.5 mol CH4 (g) or 0.5 mol of CH4 (g) would be required to produce 22 g CO2 (g).

subject: Chemistry
question_text: 50.0 kg of N 2 (g) and 10.0 kg of H 2 (g) are mixed to produce NH 3 (g). Calculate the amount of NH 3 (g) formed. Identify the limiting reagent in the production of NH 3 in this situation.
question_html: 50.0 kg of N2 (g) and 10.0 kg of H2 (g) are mixed to produce NH3 (g). Calculate the amount of NH3 (g) formed. Identify the limiting reagent in the production of NH3 in this situation.
solution_text: A balanced equation for the above reaction is written as follows: N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH 3 (g) Calculation of moles: Number of moles of N 2 = 50.0 kg N 2 {tex}\times \frac{1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{1 \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{N}_{2}} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{28.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex} = 17.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 mol Number of moles of H 2 = 10 00 kg H 2 {tex}\times \frac{1000 \mathrm{gH}_{2}}{1 \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{H}_{2}} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2}}{2.016 \mathrm{gH}_{2}}{/tex} = 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol According to the above equation, 1 mol N 2 (g) requires 3 mol H 2 (g), for the reaction. Hence, for 17.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 mol of N 2 , the moles of H 2 (g) required would be 17.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 mol N 2 {tex}\times \frac{3 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex} = 5.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol H 2 But we have only 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol H 2 . Hence, dihydrogen is the limiting reagent in this case. So, NH 3 (g) would be formed only from that amount of available dihydrogen i.e., 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol Since 3 mol H 2 (g) gives 2 mol NH 3 (g) 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol H 2 (g) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 m o l N H_{3}(g)}{3 m o l H_{2}(g)}{/tex} = 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol NH 3 (g) 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol NH 3 (g) is obtained If they are to be converted to grams, it is done as follows: 1 mol NH 3 (g) = 17.0 g NH 3 (g) 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 mol NH 3 (g) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{17.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{g})}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex} = 3.30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 17 g NH 3 (g) = 56.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 g NH 3 = 56.1 kg NH 3
solution_html:
A balanced equation for the above reaction is written as follows:
N2(g) + 3H2(g) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2NH3(g)
Calculation of moles:
Number of moles of N2
= 50.0 kg N2 {tex}\times \frac{1000 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{1 \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{N}_{2}} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{28.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex}
= 17.86{tex}\times{/tex}102 mol
Number of moles of H2
= 10 00 kg H2 {tex}\times \frac{1000 \mathrm{gH}_{2}}{1 \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{H}_{2}} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2}}{2.016 \mathrm{gH}_{2}}{/tex}
= 4.96{tex}\times{/tex}103 mol
According to the above equation, 1 mol N2 (g) requires 3 mol H2 (g), for the reaction.
Hence, for 17.86{tex}\times{/tex}102 mol of N2, the moles of H2 (g) required would be
17.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 mol N2 {tex}\times \frac{3 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex}
= 5.36 {tex}\times{/tex}103 mol H2
But we have only 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 mol H2. Hence, dihydrogen is the limiting reagent in this case. So, NH3(g) would be formed only from that amount of available dihydrogen i.e., 4.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 mol
Since 3 mol H2(g) gives 2 mol NH3(g)
4.96{tex}\times{/tex}103 mol H2 (g) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 m o l N H_{3}(g)}{3 m o l H_{2}(g)}{/tex}
= 3.30{tex}\times{/tex}103 mol NH3 (g)
3.30{tex}\times{/tex}103 mol NH3 (g) is obtained
If they are to be converted to grams, it is done as follows:
1 mol NH3 (g) = 17.0 g NH3 (g)
3.30{tex}\times{/tex}103 mol NH3 (g) {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{17.0 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{g})}{1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{g})}{/tex}
= 3.30{tex}\times{/tex}103{tex}\times{/tex}17 g NH3 (g)
= 56.1{tex}\times{/tex}103 g NH3
= 56.1 kg NH3

question_html: A solution is prepared by adding 2 g of a substance A to 18 g of water. Calculate the mass per cent of the solute.
solution_text: Mass per cent of {tex}\mathrm{A}=\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{A}}{\text { Mass of solution }} \times 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 g}{2 g \text { of } \mathrm{A}+18 \text { gof water }} \times 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 g}{20 g} \times 100{/tex} = 10 %

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the molarity of NaOH in the solution prepared by dissolving its 4 g in enough water to form 250 mL of the solution.
question_html: Calculate the molarity of NaOH in the solution prepared by dissolving its 4 g in enough water to form 250 mL of the solution.
solution_text: Since molarity (M) = {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles of solute }}{\text { Volume of solution in litres }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of NaOH / Molar mass of NaOH }}{0.250 \mathrm{L}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 g / 40 g}{0.250 L}=\frac{0.1 \mathrm{mol}}{0.250 \mathrm{L}}{/tex} = 0.4 mol -1 = 0.4 M Note that the molarity of a solution depends upon temperature because the volume of a solution is temperature-dependent.
solution_html:
Since molarity (M)
= {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles of solute }}{\text { Volume of solution in litres }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of NaOH / Molar mass of NaOH }}{0.250 \mathrm{L}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4 g / 40 g}{0.250 L}=\frac{0.1 \mathrm{mol}}{0.250 \mathrm{L}}{/tex}
= 0.4 mol-1
= 0.4 M
Note that the molarity of a solution depends upon temperature because the volume of a solution is temperature-dependent.

question_text: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is 1.25 g mL -1 . Calculate the molality of the solution.
question_html: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is 1.25 g mL-1. Calculate the molality of the solution.
solution_text: Given, Molarity of solution, M = 3 mol L -1 Mass of NaCl in 1 L solution = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 58.5 = 175.5 g Mass of 1 L solution = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} density of solution = 1000 mL {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 g/mL = 1250 g (since density = 1.25 g mL -1 ) Mass of water solution = 1250 -175.5 = 1074.5 g = 1.0745 kg. Now, Molality of solution = {tex}\frac { \text { number of moles of solute } } { \text { mass of solvent in } \mathrm { kg } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 3 \mathrm { mol } } { 1.0745 \mathrm { kg } } = 2.79 \mathrm { m }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given, Molarity of solution, M = 3 mol L-1
Mass of NaCl in 1 L solution = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 58.5 = 175.5 g
Mass of 1 L solution = Volume {tex}\times{/tex} density of solution = 1000 mL {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 g/mL = 1250 g      (since density = 1.25 g mL-1 )
Mass of water solution = 1250 -175.5 = 1074.5 g = 1.0745 kg.
Now, Molality of solution = {tex}\frac { \text { number of moles of solute } } { \text { mass of solvent in } \mathrm { kg } }{/tex}  = {tex}\frac { 3 \mathrm { mol } } { 1.0745 \mathrm { kg } } = 2.79 \mathrm { m }{/tex}.

question_text: Calculate the mass percent of different elements present in sodium sulphate (Na 2 SO 4 )
question_html: Calculate the mass percent of different elements present in sodium sulphate (Na2SO4)
solution_text: Calculations: Mass% of an element in a compound is calculated using the relation, = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of that element in the compound }}{\text { Molar mass of the compound }} \times{/tex} 100 Now the molar mass of Na 2 SO 4 = 2(23.0) + 32.0 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.0 = 142 g mol -1 So, Mass per cent of sodium = {tex}\frac{46}{142} \times{/tex} 100 = 32.39% Similarly, Mass percent of sulphur = {tex}\frac{32}{142} \times{/tex} 100 = 22.54% Mass per cent of oxygen = {tex}\frac{64}{142} \times{/tex} 100 = 45.07%
solution_html:
Calculations:
Mass% of an element in a compound is calculated using the relation,
= {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of that element in the compound }}{\text { Molar mass of the compound }} \times{/tex}100 
Now the molar mass of
Na2SO4 = 2(23.0) + 32.0 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.0
= 142 g mol-1
So, Mass per cent of sodium
= {tex}\frac{46}{142} \times{/tex}100
= 32.39%
Similarly,
Mass percent of sulphur
= {tex}\frac{32}{142} \times{/tex}100
= 22.54%
Mass per cent of oxygen
= {tex}\frac{64}{142} \times{/tex}100
= 45.07%

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Determine the empirical formula of an oxide of Iron which has 69.9° % iron and 30.1% dioxygen by mass.
question_html: Determine the empirical formula of an oxide of Iron which has 69.9° % iron and 30.1% dioxygen by mass.
solution_text: The following table illustrates different steps followed in determination of empirical formula of an oxide of iron using the given data - Element Symbol % by mass Atomic mass Mols of the element (Relative no. of moles) Simplest molar ratio simplest whole number molar ratio Iron Fe 69.9 55.85 {tex} \frac{69.9}{55.85}{/tex} = 1.25 {tex}\frac{1.25}{1.25}{/tex} = 1 2 Oxygen O 30.1 16.00 {tex}\frac{30.1}{16.00}{/tex} = 1.88 {tex}\frac{1.88}{1.25}{/tex} = 1.5 3 Thus it is concluded that the oxide contains - 2 atoms of Fe &, 3 atoms of O hence, the ratio of atoms in the oxide of iron = 2 : 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Empirical formula - Fe 2 O 3

question_text: Calculate the amount of carbon dioxide that could be produced when 1 mole of carbon is burnt in air. 1 mole of carbon is burnt in 16 g of dioxygen. 2 moles of carbon are burnt in 16 g of dioxygen.
question_html:
Calculate the amount of carbon dioxide that could be produced when

  1. 1 mole of carbon is burnt in air.
  2. 1 mole of carbon is burnt in 16 g of dioxygen.
  3. 2 moles of carbon are burnt in 16 g of dioxygen.
solution_text: The balanced equation for the combustion of carbon in dioxygen/air is {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}(s)} \\ {1 \text { mole }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g})} \\ {1 \text { mole }} \\ {\text { (32 } g )}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)} \\ {1 \text { mole }} \\ {(44 g)}\end{array}{/tex} In air, combustion is complete. Therefore, CO 2 produced from the combustion of 1 mole of carbon = 44 g. As only 16 g of dioxygen is available, it can combine only with 0.5 mole of carbon, i.e., dioxygen is the limiting reactant. Hence, CO 2 produced = 22 g. Here again, dioxygen is the limiting reactant. 16 g of dioxygen can combine only with 0.5 mole of carbon. CO 2 produced again is equal to 22 g.
solution_html:
The balanced equation for the combustion of carbon in dioxygen/air is
{tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}(s)} \\ {1 \text { mole }}\end{array}{/tex}+ {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{g})} \\ {1 \text { mole }} \\ {\text { (32 } g )}\end{array}{/tex}{tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)} \\ {1 \text { mole }} \\ {(44 g)}\end{array}{/tex}

  1. In air, combustion is complete. Therefore, CO2 produced from the combustion of 1 mole of carbon = 44 g.
  2. As only 16 g of dioxygen is available, it can combine only with 0.5 mole of carbon, i.e., dioxygen is the limiting reactant. Hence, CO2 produced = 22 g.
  3. Here again, dioxygen is the limiting reactant. 16 g of dioxygen can combine only with 0.5 mole of carbon. CO2 produced again is equal to 22 g.

question_text: Determine the molecular formula of an oxide of iron in which the mass per cent of iron and oxygen are 69.9 and 30.1 respectively.
question_html: Determine the molecular formula of an oxide of iron in which the mass per cent of iron and oxygen are 69.9 and 30.1 respectively.
solution_text: The mass per cent of iron (Fe) = 69.9 % ( given ) The mass per cent of oxygen (O) = 30.1%, ( given) Number of moles of iron present in the oxide = [69.9 / 55.85] Number of moles of oxygen present in the oxide = [30.1 / 16] The ratio of iron to oxygen in the oxide, = 1.25 : 1.88 or, = (1.25 / 1.25) : (1.88 / 1.25) = 1 : 1.5 So, a whole number ratio = 2 : 3 hence, the empirical formula of the oxide is Fe 2 O 3 The empirical formula mass of Fe 2 O 3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.85 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16.00 = 159.7 g mol -1 n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molar mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}{/tex} n = 159.69 g / 159.7g = 0.9999 = 1 (approx.) The molecular formula of a compound is obtained by multiplying the empirical formula with this positive integer (n) Thus, as per the empirical formula (Fe 2 O 3 ) of the given oxide, n = 1. Hence, the molecular formula is same as empirical formula, Fe 2 O 3 The molecular formula of the oxide is Fe 2 O 3
solution_html:
The mass per cent of iron (Fe) =  69.9 % ( given )
The mass per cent of oxygen (O) = 30.1%, ( given)
Number of moles of iron present in the oxide =  [69.9 / 55.85]
Number of moles of oxygen present in the oxide = [30.1 / 16]
The ratio of iron to oxygen in the oxide, = 1.25 : 1.88
or, = (1.25 / 1.25) : (1.88 / 1.25)
= 1 : 1.5
So, a whole number ratio
= 2 : 3
hence, the empirical formula of the oxide is Fe2O3 
The empirical formula mass of Fe2O3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.85 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex}16.00
= 159.7 g mol-1
n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molar mass }}{\text { Empirical formula mass }}{/tex}
n = 159.69 g / 159.7g = 0.9999
= 1 (approx.)
The molecular formula of a compound is obtained  by multiplying the empirical formula with this positive integer (n)
Thus, as per the empirical formula (Fe2O3 ) of the given oxide,
n = 1.
Hence, the molecular formula is same as empirical formula, Fe2O3
The molecular formula of the oxide is Fe2 O3

question_text: Calculate the atomic mass (average ) of chlorine with following data: % Natural Abundance Molar mass 35 Cl 75.77 34.9689 37 Cl 24.23 36.9659

question_text: Express the following in scientific notation: 0.0048 234,000 8008 500.0 6.0012
question_html:
Express the following in scientific notation:

  1. 0.0048

  3. 8008
  4. 500.0
  5. 6.0012
solution_text: Scientific notation is the exponential notation in which any number can be represented in the form N {tex}\times{/tex} 10 n where n is an exponent having positive or negative values and N can vary from 1 to 10. The following points are worth remembering while writing the scientific notation for a number. You move the decimal point of a number until the new form is a number up to 10 ( but not 10 itself ) and then record the exponent (n) as the number of places the decimal was removed. Whether the power of 10 is positive or negative depends on whether you move the decimal to the right or to the left. Moving the decimal to the right makes the exponent negative and moving to the left gives you a positive exponent. Accordingly, the scientific notation of given numbers are - 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 2.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 8.008 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 5.000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 6.0012 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 0

solution_text: The distance covered is calculated using the relation, Speed = {tex}\frac {\text{Distance covered}}{\text{time taken}}{/tex} or, distance covered = Speed {tex}\times{/tex} time Substituting the given values of speed = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 & time taken = 2.00 ns, we get Distance covered = speed {tex}\times{/tex} times = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.00 ns (Since 1.00 ns = 10 -9 sec), therefore = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.00 ns {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{10^{-9} s}{1 \mathrm{ns}}{/tex} = 6.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 m or = 0.600 m

question_text: In a reaction A + B 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} AB 2 Identify the limiting reagent if any in the, following reaction mixtures: 300 atoms of A + 200 molecules of B 2 mol of A + 3 mol of B 100 atoms of A + 100 molecules of B 5 mol of A + 2.5 mol of B 2.5 mol of A + 5 mol of B
question_html:
In a reaction
A + B2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} AB2
Identify the limiting reagent if any in the, following reaction mixtures:

  1. 300 atoms of A + 200 molecules of B
  2. 2 mol of A + 3 mol of B
  3. 100 atoms of A + 100 molecules of B
  4. 5 mol of A + 2.5 mol of B
  5. 2.5 mol of A + 5 mol of B
solution_text: According to the following, 1 mol of A reacted with 1 mole of B 2 and 1 atom of A reacts with 1 molecule of B 2 . B is limiting reagent because of 200 molecules of B 2 will react with 200 atoms of A and 100 atoms of A will be left in excess. A is limiting reagent because 2 moles of A will react with 2 mol of B and 1 mol of B will be left in excess. Both will react completely because it is a stoichiometric mixture. No limiting reagent. 2.5 mol of B will react with 2.5 mol of A and hence B is limiting reagent. 2.5 mol of A will react with 2.5 mol of B. Hence A is limiting reagent.
solution_html:
According to the following,
1 mol of A reacted with 1 mole of B2 and 1 atom of A reacts with 1 molecule of B2.

  1. B is limiting reagent because of 200 molecules of B2 will react with 200 atoms of A and 100 atoms of A will be left in excess.
  2. A is limiting reagent because 2 moles of A will react with 2 mol of B and 1 mol of B will be left in excess.
  3. Both will react completely because it is a stoichiometric mixture. No limiting reagent.
  4. 2.5 mol of B will react with 2.5 mol of A and hence B is limiting reagent.
  5. 2.5 mol of A will react with 2.5 mol of B. Hence A is limiting reagent.

question_text: Dinitrogen and dihydrogen react with each other to produce ammonia according to the following chemical equation: N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NH 3 (g) Calculate the mass of ammonia produced if 2.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 g dinitrogen reacts with 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 g dihydrogen Will any of the two reactants remain unreacted? If yes, which one and what would be its mass?
question_html:
Dinitrogen and dihydrogen react with each other to produce ammonia according to the following chemical equation:
N2 (g) + 3H2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NH3 (g)

  1. Calculate the mass of ammonia produced if 2.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 g dinitrogen reacts with 1.00 {tex}\times{/tex}103 g dihydrogen
  2. Will any of the two reactants remain unreacted?
  3. If yes, which one and what would be its mass?
solution_text: As per given equation , N 2(g) + 3 H 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 NH 3 (g) Stoichiometrically, 1 mol of N 2 ( i.e. = 28 g) react with 3 mols of H 2 , ( i.e. = 6 g of H 2 ) 2000 g of N 2 will react with [ (6/28) (2000) ] = 428.6 g.of H 2. Thus, N 2 is the limiting reagent while H 2 is the excess reagent. Again, 1 mol. of B N 2 , ( i.e. 28 g of N 2 produce ) NH 3 = 2 mole (ie. 34 g of NH 3 (g) {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2000 g of N 2 will produce = [(34 / 28) {tex}\times{/tex} (2000)] g = 2428.57 g H 2 will remain unreacted , because 1000 g of N 2 has been taken for reaction, out of which only 428.6 g is consumed. Mass of H 2 left unreacted = ( 1000 g - 428.6 g ) = 571.4 g
solution_html:
As per given equation  ,
N2(g) + 3 H2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 NH3 (g) 
Stoichiometrically,

  1. 1 mol of N2 ( i.e. = 28 g) react with 3 mols of H2, ( i.e. = 6 g of H2)
    2000 g of N2 will react with [ (6/28) (2000) ]
    = 428.6 g.of H2.
    Thus, N2 is the limiting reagent while H2 is the excess reagent.
    Again,
    1 mol. of B N2, ( i.e. 28 g of  N2 produce ) NH3
    = 2 mole (ie. 34 g of NH3 (g)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  2000 g of N2 will produce
    = [(34 / 28) {tex}\times{/tex} (2000)] g
    = 2428.57 g
  2. H2 will remain unreacted,
    because 1000 g of N2 has been taken for reaction, out of which only 428.6 g is consumed. 
  3. Mass of H2 left unreacted
    = ( 1000 g - 428.6 g )
    = 571.4 g

question_text: How are 0.50 mol Na 2 CO 3 and 0.50 M Na 2 CO 3 different?
question_html: How are 0.50 mol Na2CO3 and 0.50 M Na2CO3 different?
solution_text: Molar mass of Na 2 CO 3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 23 + 12 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 106 g mol -1 Now , 1 mole of Na 2 CO 3 means 106 gms of Na 2 CO 3 0.50 mol Na 2 CO 3 means 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 53 gms of Na 2 CO 3 0.50 M Na 2 CO 3 means 0.50 mol, i.e. 53 g Na 2 CO 3 are present in 1 litre of the solution.
solution_html:
Molar mass of Na2CO3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 23 + 12 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 106 g mol-1
Now , 1 mole of Na2 CO3 means 106 gms of Na2 CO3 
0.50 mol Na2CO3 means 106 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 53 gms of Na2CO3
0.50 M Na2CO3 means 0.50 mol, i.e. 53 g Na2CO3 are present in 1 litre of the solution.

question_text: If ten volumes of dihydrogen gas react with five volumes of dioxygen gas, how many volumes of water vapour would be produced?
question_html: If ten volumes of dihydrogen gas react with five volumes of dioxygen gas, how many volumes of water vapour would be produced?
solution_text: H 2 and O 2 react according to the equation 2 H 2 (g) + O 2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 H 2 O (g) Thus, 2 volumes of H 2 react with 1 volume of O 2 to produce 2 volumes of water vapour. Hence, 10 volumes of H 2 will react completely with 5 volumes of O 2 to produce 10 volumes of water vapour.
solution_html:
H2 and O2 react according to the equation
2 H2 (g) + O2 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 H2O (g)
Thus, 2 volumes of H2 react with 1 volume of O2 to produce 2 volumes of water vapour.
Hence, 10 volumes of H2 will react completely with 5 volumes of O2 to produce 10 volumes of water vapour.

question_text: Calculate the molarity of a solution of ethanol in water, in which the mole fraction of ethanol is 0.040 (assume the density of water to be one).
question_html: Calculate the molarity of a solution of ethanol in water, in which the mole fraction of ethanol is 0.040 (assume the density of water to be one).
solution_text: Let the mole fraction of ethanol (C 2 H 5 OH) be represented by x, Then the following expression represents its mole fraction in ethanol solution- x C 2 h 5 OH = {tex}\frac{{n}\left({C}_{2} \ {H}_{5} {OH}\right)}{{n}\left({C}_{2} \ {H}_{5} \ {OH}\right)+\ {n}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)}{/tex} (where x C 2 H 5 OH represents mole fraction of ethanol) = 0.040 (given) ...(i); where "n'' (represents number of moles of the two referred liquids in ethanol solution). The following steps are used for calculations, The aim is to find number of moles of ethanol in 1 L of the solution. Step 1- No. of moles in 1 L of water = {tex}\frac{1000 \mathrm \ {g}}{18 \mathrm \ {g} \mathrm \ {mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.55 moles Step 2- Substituting n (H 2 O) = 55.55 in equation (i), we get x ( C 2 H 5 OH ) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)}{\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)+55.55}{/tex} = 0.040 ...(given) solving for n (C 2 H 5 OH) we get, 0.96 n (C 2 H 5 OH) = 55.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.040 {tex}\therefore{/tex} n (C 2 H 5 OH) = 2.31 mols So 2.31 moles of C 2 H 5 OH are present per litre of given ethanol solution. Hence, the molarity of the solution of ethanol is 2.31 M.

x C2h5OH = {tex}\frac{{n}\left({C}_{2} \ {H}_{5} {OH}\right)}{{n}\left({C}_{2} \ {H}_{5} \ {OH}\right)+\ {n}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)}{/tex}
(where x C2H5OH  represents mole fraction of ethanol)
= 0.040 (given) ...(i);
where "n'' (represents number of moles of the two referred liquids in ethanol solution).
The following steps are used for calculations,
The aim is to find number of moles of ethanol in 1 L of the solution.
Step 1-  No. of moles in 1 L of water
= {tex}\frac{1000 \mathrm \ {g}}{18 \mathrm \ {g} \mathrm \ {mol}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 55.55 moles
Step 2- Substituting n (H2O)
= 55.55 in equation (i), we get
x ( C2H5OH)
= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)}{\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}\right)+55.55}{/tex}
= 0.040 ...(given)
solving for n (C2H5OH) we get,
0.96 n (C2 H5 OH)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} n (C2H5OH)

question_text: A welding fuel gas contains carbon and hydrogen only. Burning a small sample of it in oxygen gives 3.38 g carbon dioxide, 0.690 g of water and no other products. A volume of 10.0 L (measured at S.T.P) of this welding gas is found to weigh 11.6 g. Calculate empirical formula, molar mass of the gas, and molecular formula.
question_html:
A welding fuel gas contains carbon and hydrogen only. Burning a small sample of it in oxygen gives 3.38 g carbon dioxide, 0.690 g of water and no other products. A volume of 10.0 L (measured at S.T.P) of this welding gas is found to weigh 11.6 g.
Calculate

  1. empirical formula,
  2. molar mass of the gas, and
  3. molecular formula.
solution_text: Amount of carbon in 3.38 g of CO 2 = {tex}\frac{12}{44} \times{/tex} 3.38 g = 0.9218 g Amount of hydrogen in 0.690 g of H2O = {tex}\frac{2}{18} \times{/tex} 0.690 g = 0.0767 g As compound contains only C and H, therefore, the total mass of the compound = 0.9218 + 0.0767 g = 0.9985 g % of C in the compound = {tex}\frac{0.9218}{0.9985} \times{/tex} 100 = 92.32 % of H in the compound = {tex}\frac{0.0767}{0.9985} \times{/tex} 100 = 7.68 Calculation of Empirical Formula Element % bt mass Atomic mass Moles of the element Simplest molar ratio Simplest whole no. molar ratio C 92.32 12 {tex}\frac{92.32}{12}{/tex} = 7.69 1 1 H 7.68 1 {tex}\frac{7.68}{1}{/tex} = 7.68 1 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Empirical formula = CH Hence, the empirical formula of the gas is 'CH' Given, 10.0 L of the gas at STP weighs = 11.6 g {tex}\therefore{/tex} 22.4 L of the gas at S.T.P. will weigh = {tex}\frac{11.6}{10.0} \times{/tex} 22.4 = 25.984 g = 26 g (approx.) Hence, the molar mass of the gas = 26 g mol -1 Empirical formula mass of CH = 12 + 1 = 13, (calculated as above) {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { E.F.Mass }}=\frac{26}{13}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular formula = (CH)n or, = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} CH = C 2 H 2
solution_html:
Amount of carbon in 3.38 g of CO2 = {tex}\frac{12}{44} \times{/tex}3.38 g = 0.9218 g
Amount of hydrogen in 0.690 g of H2O = {tex}\frac{2}{18} \times{/tex}0.690 g = 0.0767 g
As compound contains only C and H, therefore, the total mass of the compound
= 0.9218 + 0.0767 g = 0.9985 g
% of C in the compound = {tex}\frac{0.9218}{0.9985} \times{/tex}100 = 92.32
% of H in the compound = {tex}\frac{0.0767}{0.9985} \times{/tex} 100 = 7.68

  1. Calculation of Empirical Formula
    Element % bt mass Atomic mass Moles of the
    element    		 Simplest molar
    ratio    		 Simplest whole
    no. molar ratio
    C 92.32 12 {tex}\frac{92.32}{12}{/tex} = 7.69 1 1
    H 7.68 1 {tex}\frac{7.68}{1}{/tex} = 7.68 1 1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Empirical formula = CH
    Hence, the empirical  formula of the gas is 'CH'
  2. Given,
    10.0 L of the gas at STP weighs = 11.6 g
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 22.4 L of the gas at S.T.P. will weigh = {tex}\frac{11.6}{10.0} \times{/tex}22.4 = 25.984 g = 26 g (approx.)
    Hence, the molar mass of the gas = 26 g mol-1
  3. Empirical formula mass of CH = 12 + 1 = 13, (calculated as above)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { E.F.Mass }}=\frac{26}{13}{/tex}= 2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular formula = (CH)n
    or, = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} CH = C2H2

question_text: Calcium carbonate reacts with aqueous HCl to give CaCl 2 and CO 2 according to the reaction, CaCO 3 (s) + 2 HCl (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCl 2 (aq) + CO 2 (g) + H 2 O (l) What mass of CaCO 3 is required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl?
question_html:
Calcium carbonate reacts with aqueous HCl to give CaCl2 and CO2 according to the reaction, CaCO3 (s) + 2 HCl (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCl2 (aq) + CO2 (g) + H2O (l)
What mass of CaCO3 is required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl?
solution_text: Step 1. To calculate mass of HCl in 25 mL of 0.75 m HCl 1000 mL of 0.75 M HCl contain HCl = 0.75 mol = 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 36.5 g (molar mass of HCl = 36.5) = 24.375 g {tex}\therefore{/tex} 25 mL of 0.75 HCl will contain HCl = {tex}\frac{24.375}{1000} \times{/tex} 25g = 0.6844 g Step 2. To calculate mass of CaCO 3 reacting completely with 0.9125 g of HCl CaCO 3 (s) + 2HCl (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCl 2 (aq) + CO 2 (g) + H 2 O(l) Using stoichiometric relations in the above-balanced equation we infer that, 2 mol of HCl ie. = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 36.5 g = 73 g HCl react completely with caCO 3 (s) = 1 mol = 100 g; (since molar mass of CaCO 3 is 100) {tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.6844 g HCl will react completely with CaCO 3 = {tex}\frac{100}{73} \times{/tex} 0.6844 g = 0.938 g Thus, the mass of CaCO 3 required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl is 0.938 g.
solution_html:
Step 1. To calculate mass of HCl in 25 mL of 0.75 m HCl
1000 mL of 0.75 M HCl contain HCl = 0.75 mol
= 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 36.5 g (molar mass of HCl = 36.5) 
= 24.375 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 25 mL of 0.75 HCl will contain HCl = {tex}\frac{24.375}{1000} \times{/tex}25g = 0.6844 g
Step 2. To calculate mass of CaCO3 reacting completely with 0.9125 g of HCl
CaCO3 (s) + 2HCl (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CaCl2 (aq) + CO2 (g) + H2O(l)
Using stoichiometric relations in the above-balanced equation we  infer that,
2 mol of HCl ie. = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 36.5 g = 73 g HCl react completely with caCO3 (s) = 1 mol = 100 g; (since molar mass of CaCO3 is 100)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.6844 g HCl will react completely with CaCO3 = {tex}\frac{100}{73} \times{/tex} 0.6844 g = 0.938 g
Thus, the mass of CaCO3  required to react completely with 25 mL of 0.75 M HCl is 0.938 g.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Chlorine is prepared in the laboratory by treating manganese dioxide (MnO 2 ) with aqueous hydrochloric acid according to the reaction 4HCl(aq) + MnO 2 (s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H 2 O(l) + MnCI 2 (aq) + CI 2 (g) How many grams of HCI react with 5.0 g of manganese dioxide?
question_html:
Chlorine is prepared in the laboratory by treating manganese dioxide (MnO2) with aqueous hydrochloric acid according to the reaction
4HCl(aq) + MnO2(s) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H2O(l) + MnCI2(aq) + CI2(g)
How many grams of HCI react with 5.0 g of manganese dioxide?
solution_text: The given chemical equation is: {tex}\mathop {4HCl(aq)}\limits_{4 \times 36.5} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{87g} \rightarrow 2{H_2}O(l) + MnC{l_2}(aq) + C{l_2}(g){/tex} It is clear from balanced chemical equation, 87 g of MnO 2 reacts with 146 g HCI Therefore, 5 g of MnO 2 will react with = {tex}\frac{146\;\times5}{87}{/tex} = 8.4 g HCl.
solution_html:
The given chemical equation is:
{tex}\mathop {4HCl(aq)}\limits_{4 \times 36.5} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{87g} \rightarrow 2{H_2}O(l) + MnC{l_2}(aq) + C{l_2}(g){/tex}
It is clear from balanced chemical equation,
87 g of MnO2 reacts with 146 g HCI
Therefore, 5 g of MnO2 will react with = {tex}\frac{146\;\times5}{87}{/tex} = 8.4 g HCl.
